food_name: Wonton Noodle Soup (Hong Kong Cantonese Style)

local_names.romanized_local: Yún tūn miàn / Wan4 tan1 min6 (Cantonese)

cuisine: Cantonese — Hong Kong Style — Singapore Hawker
region_of_origin: Guangdong (Cantonese), Hong Kong; widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Cantonese noodle soup — thin springy kansui-alkalised egg noodles with pork-prawn wontons, char siu, and clear pork-prawn broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Wonton Noodle Soup as a prepared Cantonese hawker dish. Closest parent would be a noodle soup composite food; no exact URI available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Egg noodle soup with wonton and pork (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent for egg noodle soup with pork components. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Hong Kong-style wonton noodle soup as a composite hawker dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code available for this prepared composite hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl (~620g) comprising approximately 120g cooked thin egg noodles, 6 pork-prawn wontons (~120g), ~300ml clear pork-prawn broth, 2 slices char siu (~40g), and choy sum (~50g).

nutrient_source.title: Wonton Noodle Soup — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database for wonton noodle soup components (egg noodles, wontons, char siu, choy sum, broth) and aggregated by component weight to produce per-100g composite values for a 620g bowl. Values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from enriched JSON estimates (confidence 0.9).

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Egg Noodles (al dente, thin, wheat-egg); analogous estimate for HK wonton noodle soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~55 assigned based on thin al dente egg noodles (kansui-alkalised) as the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database. Egg noodles have a lower GI than plain wheat noodles due to egg protein content. Fat from char siu and broth, and protein from wonton filling, further moderate the glycaemic response. Available carbohydrate per serving ~58g (noodles + wonton skin); GL = 55 × 58 / 100 ≈ 31.9. GI level classified as 'low' as 55 falls within the low GI range (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,050mg per bowl, ~169mg per 100g) from broth, char siu, wonton seasoning, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit wonton noodle soup to occasional consumption; request reduced-broth or dry (kon lo) version to lower sodium by ~300mg, avoid adding side soy sauce, and leave broth unfinished to reduce sodium intake by a further 150–250mg per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) but high glycaemic load (~32 per bowl) driven by ~58g available carbohydrate from thin egg noodles and wonton skins
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully; the thin al dente egg noodles have a moderately lower GI than thick wheat noodles, and the protein from wonton filling (~14g) and fat from char siu help moderate the glycaemic response, but the total GL of ~32 per bowl is high and requires carbohydrate budgeting within the daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~1,050mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~7g per bowl, primarily from char siu and pork wonton filling)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to high sodium and moderate saturated fat; choosing the dry version with sauce on the side, reducing char siu portions, and not finishing the broth are practical strategies to reduce cardiovascular risk per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate sodium and saturated fat; choy sum provides anti-inflammatory micronutrients (Vitamin C ~15mg, folate)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients in moderate portions; choy sum contributes Vitamin C and folate which support immune function, but the high sodium content warrants caution for those on sodium-restricted anti-inflammatory diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wonton Noodle Soup (云吞面 / 雲吞麵) is a defining dish of Hong Kong Cantonese cuisine, widely popular in Singapore and Malaysia as a hawker staple. The dish consists of thin, springy egg noodles made with duck egg and kansui (alkaline solution), which gives them their characteristic yellow colour, firm 'al dente' bite, and slightly lower glycaemic index compared to plain wheat noodles. A standard Singapore hawker bowl (~620g) contains approximately 120g cooked thin egg noodles, 6 pork-prawn wontons (~120g, filled with minced pork and whole or minced prawn), ~300ml clear pork-prawn broth, 2 slices of char siu (Cantonese BBQ pork, ~40g), and blanched choy sum (~50g). The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Nutritionally, one bowl provides approximately 550 kcal, 28g protein, 18g fat (7g saturated), 60g carbohydrate, and ~1,050mg sodium — the sodium content is HIGH and is the primary clinical concern. GI is approximately 55 (low), with a glycaemic load of ~32 per bowl. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork broth and char siu) and is NOT suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is dairy-free. Key allergens include gluten (wheat noodles, wonton skin), egg, crustaceans (prawn), and soy. In Singapore, it is commonly found at hawker centres (Old Airport Road, Bedok 85, Amoy Street Food Centre), Tim Ho Wan, and Crystal Jade. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database via component calculation for a standard Singapore hawker wonton noodle soup bowl. HPB Singapore is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are complete. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (thin al dente egg noodles). Minor fields (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched JSON at confidence 0.9.
notes: 1. GI level in input was incorrectly labelled 'low'; corrected to 'low' (GI 55 is within the low GI range ≤55). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for this dish type; no industrial trans fats in traditional Cantonese noodle preparation. 3. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by HPB Foodo for this composite dish; set to -1.0. 4. water_g not reported by source; set to -1.0. 5. FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for HK-style wonton noodle soup. 6. Hindi alias 'वॉन्टन नूडल सूप' added to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 7. Sodium per bowl ~1,050mg is HIGH; primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
